data.title: US imposes sanctions on Sudan's finance minister, Treasury Department website shows
data.description:
The United States has imposed sanctions on Sudan's finance minister, Jibril Ibrahim, according to a notice posted to the U.S. Treasury Department's website on Friday.

The notice also showed that Sudanese Islamist militia al-Baraa Ibn Malik brigade was designated under new Sudan-related sanctions.

data.title: Frequency Electronics Slips After 1Q Profit Falls
data.description:
By Dean Seal

Shares of Frequency Electronic sank after the company reported lower revenue and a big drop in earnings for the latest completed quarter.

The stock was down 16%, to $28.69, in Friday afternoon trading. Shares have still gained 55% since the start of the year.

The maker of timing- and frequency-control products for defense and commercial customers said after Thursday's closing bell that revenue for the fiscal first quarter ended July 31 declined 8.4%, to $13.8 million.

Chief Executive Thomas McClelland blamed the shortfall on program delays from a few key customers, as congressional allocations for space- and defense-related programs weren't finalized until early July, with just a few weeks left in the quarter.

That delayed revenue should still be earned in the coming quarters, and at least one of the programs is expected to see a meaningful increase in contract value, the CEO said on a call with analysts on Thursday.

Despite the drop in revenue, cost of revenue was up 4%, slashing the company's gross margin. Overhead costs meanwhile surged 26%.

Frequency Electronics walked away from the quarter with earnings of $634,000, or 7 cents a share. That is down from $2.4 million, or 25 cents a share, in the same quarter a year earlier.

McClelland told analysts the company is investing in growth and will therefore "see temporarily lower levels of profitability."

Write to Dean Seal at dean.seal@wsj.com

data.title: TON Strategy launches $250M buyback while shares drop 7.5%
data.description:
TON Strategy Company, previously known as Verb Technology Company, said on Friday it repurchased 250,000 shares of its common stock under its $250 million buyback agreement.

According to the announcement, TON Strategy Company repurchased the shares at $8.32 each, below the treasury asset value of $12.18. The buyback comes just weeks after it revealed a $713 million reserve of Toncoin (TON) tokens on Aug. 21. TON Strategy says its buyback indicates “long-term confidence” in the TON ecosystem.

The TON token is the native cryptocurrency of The Open Network, a project that started in 2018 and was initially conceived to bring blockchain to messaging service Telegram. The crypto token is ranked No. 22 overall in market capitalization, with its price is declining 40.7% year-to-date, .

The repurchases come as the company’s share price has fallen 21.6% since adopting the TON reserve treasury.

TON Strategy’s shares declined 7.5% on the buyback announcement Friday.

The company also announced the beginning of its staking operations to generate onchain income from its treasury holdings.

“Staking introduces a recurring revenue stream into our model, while buybacks allow us to enhance shareholder returns,” TON Strategy Company CEO Veronika Kapustina said in a statement.

Staking is the process of locking up cryptocurrency to support a blockchain network’s operations, such as validating transactions, in exchange for rewards. According to Staking Rewards data, there are 340 validators actively staking on the TON network, with annual reward rates at 4.8% as of this writing.

TON treasury companies and other DATs

TON Strategy Company became the first publicly traded company to start a Toncoin treasury in August. In July, Bloomberg reported that the TON Foundation and Kingsway Capital Partners, a London-based investment management firm, sought to raise at least $400 million to start a TON treasury company.

Crypto exchange Coinbase recently said that crypto treasury companies are entering a “player vs player” stage where investor money will become harder to find.

The New York Digital Investment Group said Sunday that premiums are narrowing for such companies and may continue to do so.

data.title: Adobe Seen Reaching Turning Point in AI Cycle — Market Talk
data.description: Adobe's F3Q results mark a turning point, with key growth metrics in its core and new AI-first products trending positively, BofA Securities analysts say in a note. The report should help more investors see Adobe as an AI beneficiary, though a debate over whether it's an AI winner or loser is likely to continue, the analysts say. Going forward, gradual, continued traction with AI-first products will likely drive reaccelerating growth, the analysts say, estimating an incremental 1% point of topline growth in fiscal 2027, assuming it maintains its current quarterly AI net new annual recurring revenue run rate. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Arabica coffee hits four-month high, sugar and cocoa ease
data.description:
Arabica coffee futures on ICE rose to a four-month high on Friday boosted by tight supplies in the U.S. following the imposition of a 50% tariff on shipments from Brazil.

COFFEE

* Arabica coffee settled up 10.75 cents, or 2.8%, at $3.9685 per lb after climbing to a four-month high of $3.9855. Arabica gained 6.2% in the week.

* "The main drivers of bullish sentiment continue to be concerns that the Brazilian coffee harvest will be smaller than initially expected and the U.S. imposition of 50% tariffs on Brazilian exports, contributing to low stocks in the U.S.," BMI analyst said in a note.

* Brazil's coffee exports to the United States in August fell 46% from a year earlier after the Trump administration's 50% tariff on imports from Brazil.

* Robusta coffee rose 1.8% to $4,601 a metric ton.

SUGAR

* Raw sugar ​​settled down 0.03 cents, or 0.2%, at 15.79 cents per lb, though the market posted a weekly gain of 1.5% after two successive weekly declines.

* Dealers said recent weakness in sugar prices could encourage Brazilian mills to consider decreasing the proportion of cane used to make the sweetener. Cane can also be used to produce bioethanol.

* Indonesia has halted issuance of raw sugar import permits for the rest of the year, Trade Minister Budi Santoso said on Friday, after sugarcane farmers complained about difficulties in competing with imports.

* White sugar fell 0.6% to $485.20 a ton, with the focus on Monday's expiry of the October contract.

* Dealers said about 220,000 tons of sugar were expected to be delivered against the contract.

* India, the world's second-biggest sugar producer, will have sufficient surplus stocks to allow exports in the season beginning on October 1, a government official said on Friday.

COCOA

* London cocoa settled down 90 pounds, or 1.7%, at 5,106 pounds per ton.

* New York cocoa lost 1.4% to $7,420 a ton.

* Despite the negative Friday session, New York cocoa could test resistance at $7,747 a metric ton, a break above which could open the way towards a range of $7,867 to $8,063, said Reuters technical analyst Wang Tao.

data.title: LA County reports child died from rare measles complication after infection in infancy
data.description:
Los Angeles County health officials reported the death of a school-aged resident from a known but rare complication after a measles infection acquired during infancy, and urged residents to ensure their families are vaccinated against the disease.

WHY IT'S IMPORTANT

The Los Angeles County Department of Public Health said on Thursday that the child was first infected with measles as an infant before they were eligible to receive the measles vaccine, which is routinely recommended to be administered between 12 and 15 months of age.

Although the child recovered from the initial measles illness, they developed and ultimately died from subacute sclerosing panencephalitis, or SSPE — a rare, progressive brain disorder that can be a late complication of a measles infection early in life.

BY THE NUMBERS

SSPE affects about one in 10,000 measles cases, but the risk increases to one in 600 for infants infected before they become eligible for vaccination, according to LA County health officials.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has reported 1,454 confirmed measles cases nationwide this year, as of September 9.

CONTEXT

Measles is a highly contagious viral infection that causes symptoms such as fever, cough and a characteristic rash. It can also lead to severe complications like pneumonia and encephalitis.

The U.S. recorded its highest measles case count since 2000 earlier this year, with Texas and New Mexico driving a January surge due to declining vaccination rates among children.

There have been eight cases of measles in individuals who were in Los Angeles County while infectious, including four residents and four non-residents in 2025.

WHAT'S NEXT

Health officials are urging residents to check immunization records, vaccinate eligible family members, and take precautions, particularly before traveling to areas with measles outbreaks.

data.title: Edwards Lifesciences on Track for Longest Losing Streak Since August 2023 — Data Talk
data.description:
Edwards Lifesciences Corporation (EW) is currently at $76.97, down $1.04 or 1.33%

  • Would be lowest close since Aug. 12, 2025, when it closed at $76.93
  • Currently down eight consecutive days; down 5.87% over this period
  • Longest losing streak since Aug. 24, 2023, when it fell for eight straight trading days
  • Worst eight-day stretch since the eight days ending Feb. 27, 2025, when it fell 6.29%
  • Down 4.90% this week; worst weekly performance since the week ending Jan. 17, 2025, when it fell 4.96%
  • Down 5.37% month-to-date
  • Up 3.97% year-to-date
  • Down 41.1% from its all-time closing high of $130.68 on Dec. 30, 2021
  • Up 12.33% from 52 weeks ago (Sept. 13, 2024), when it closed at $68.52
  • Down 5.94% from its 52-week closing high of $81.83 on Aug. 22, 2025
  • Up 19.26% from its 52-week closing low of $64.54 on Oct. 7, 2024
  • Traded as low as $76.49; lowest intraday level since Aug. 12, 2025, when it hit $76.07
  • Down 1.95% at today's intraday low

All data as of 2:24:51 PM ET

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: Sugar Futures Edges Higher on USDA Projection
data.description:
Sugar futures edged above 15.8 cents per pound as the USDA released its latest supply and demand report.

For 2024/25, US sugar supply slipped to 14.716 million short tons, as a drop in beet sugar production outweighed higher imports.

Beet sugar output fell 83,500 STRV due to a smaller-than-expected crop, while re-export and high-tier imports rose slightly.

With sugar use unchanged, ending stocks increased modestly.

Looking to 2025/26, US sugar supply is projected to decline 184,000 STRV, mainly from lower imports from Mexico, despite modest gains in beet and cane sugar production driven by higher yields in some regions.

The Department of Commerce will use the outlook to set Mexico’s export limit starting October 1, projected at 219,638 STRV. Overall, the report points to tighter US sugar supplies, with production and import adjustments keeping the market balanced.

data.title: Union Pacific CEO says he had talks with Trump on $85 billion plan to buy Norfolk
data.description:
Union Pacific Corp CEO Jim Vena met with U.S. President Donald Trump in the Oval Office to discuss the railroad's proposed $85 billion buyout of Norfolk Southern Corp , the company said on Friday, as it awaits regulatory approval for what could be the U.S. freight rail industry's most significant consolidation in decades.

White House support could accelerate the lengthy review process despite resistance from rivals and pushback from shippers concerned about reduced competition.

The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The merger, if approved, could reshape the U.S. freight rail industry by creating the first coast-to-coast single-line network, streamlining operations and eliminating interchange delays in key hubs like Chicago.

According to a company statement, Trump and Vena discussed "how creating an American transcontinental railroad is a win for U.S. competition, consumers, and the unionized workers whose jobs will be protected when the merger is approved."

At a Morgan Stanley conference on Wednesday, Vena said he had meetings with senior administration officials, without naming them. "They see the strategic value in what we're proposing. It's a clear win for the country," he said.

data.title: U.S. Oil Rig Count Rises By 2 to 416 — Market Talk
data.description:
1433 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose for a third consecutive week, increasing by two to 416, or 72 fewer than a year ago, Baker Hughes reports. Rigs directed at natural gas were unchanged this week at 118, up by 21 from a year earlier. The EIA expects drilling activity in 2026 to be more centered in natural gas-intensive regions given its forecasts for lower oil but higher natural gas prices. "We forecast that crude oil will trade at its lowest premium to natural gas since 2005," the EIA said in its latest Short Term Energy Outlook. "We expect decreases in natural gas produced as a by-product of oil directed drilling will offset increases in that produced by natural gas-directed drilling." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1426 ET - A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery makes sense strategically, but a full integration would take time, BofA Securities analysts say in a note. They estimate that integration and restructuring under the combined company would likely take years to implement, pointing to the lengthy process that came with Warner Bros.' combination with Discovery. That said, the deal would significantly boost Paramount's prospects, particularly in film and television, given access to Warner Bros.' best-in-class library, they say. It would also create a formidable competitor in streaming with the potential combination of Paramount+ and HBO Max, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1421 ET - Inflation-adjusted investment in Canada on industrial machinery and equipment fell in 2Q to its lowest level in over 40 years, according to calculations from National Bank Financial. The decline has been so pronounced that Canada is at risk "of becoming irrelevant in global supply chains," the firm's economists say. "The divergence with the U.S. is nothing short of appalling." The Mark Carney-led Liberal government is trying to reverse this trend, through accelerated defense spending, and trying to fast track infrastructure and resource projects. "But time is of the essence." NBF says the drop to historically low business-investment levels can be attributed to excessive regulation, and a "chronic lack of ambition" in Ottawa from promoting the country's resource base. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1419 ET - 2025 will mark the end of one of Bank of America's biggest investment themes for this decade: the trend of moving away from Chinese assets known as the "Anywhere But China" trade, the bank's analysts say in a research note. Amid the AI bubble in the U.S., investors should take a "barbell" approach to investing, with capital flowing to a mix of low-risk and high-risk global technology investments, the analysts say. China and Japan are bright spots in this new paradigm, they say. Chinese technology companies are an optimal way to barbell the U.S. AI bubble, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1312 ET - Most of Boeing's 3,200 St. Louis-area union machinists voted Friday to keep picketing, rejecting a new five-year contract proposal that their negotiators had agreed to earlier in the week. "Our members will always have the final say in their futures," union representative Tom Boelling said. The vote adds pressure on Boeing's defense segment, which works on fighter jets and munitions in the region. ( andrew.fitzgerald@wsj.com ; @drewfitzgerald)

1309 ET - The USDA raises the forecast for U.S. cotton production in the current marketing year, "with no change to exports, consumption, imports, or stocks," the agency says in the September WASDE report. Output is estimated at 13.22 million bales, up from 13.21 million bales in the previous forecast. Ending stocks remain at 3.6 million bales. World production projection is raised to 117.68 million bales from 116.62 million bales, with stocks projection down to 73.14 from 73.91 million bales. The USDA says U.S. planted area has increased slightly. On the CBOT, cotton is slightly higher than yesterday, losing some steam after the WASDE report. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1246 ET - Beef will remain scarce and expensive into 2026, the USDA indicates in the September WASDE report, citing a slower rate of slaughter. Beef production in 2026 is projected at 25.49 billion pounds, little changed from the August estimate and down from a downwardly revised 25.83 billion pounds forecast for 2025. Steers price estimates for 2026 rise to $2.49 from $2.44 a pound. Pork production in 2026 remains at 28.38 billion pounds, up from downwardly revised 27.57 billion pounds 2025 estimate. Barrows and gilts 2026 price estimate is kept at 66 cents a pound. Livestock futures are little changed, with live cattle falling 0.8% and lean hogs down 1.2%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1211 ET - Amazon Autos hasn't hit top gear yet, especially in used vehicles, say Davidson analysts Tom White and Wyatt Swanson. Amazon Autos was launched in 2023 with a partnership with Hyundai for new vehicles, but the analysts say that Hyundai dealers are still seeing very limited sales through Amazon. Early last month, Amazon said dealers would be able to list their used vehicles on Amazon Autos, but " there remains relatively scant Used inventory available on the Amazon Autos platform," the analysts say. Almost all of the used inventory that is available is from Hertz, which recently said it was partnering with Amazon, and participating Hyundai dealers, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1156 ET - The estimated funding level of pension plans sponsored by S&P 1500 companies increased 1 percentage point in August to 109% as a result of an increase in domestic equities, according to consulting firm Mercer LLC. As of the end of August, the plans' estimated aggregate surplus increased by $20 billion, to $136 billion, compared with a $116 billion surplus at the end of July, Mercer said. "We saw the interest rate curve steepen, with long-term rates rising and short-term rates falling, which could have various impacts on companies within the S&P 1500 depending on the duration of their plans," said Mercer partner Scott Jarboe. (jennifer.williams@wsj.com; @jenkayw)

1126 ET - Tariffs were expected to hit the auto industry hard, "yet six months into the onset of tariffs, we've been positively surprised by the extent to which the industry has held in better than anticipated," Barclays analysts Dan Levy and Josh Cho say in a research note, citing minimal disruption to production and sales, as well as ongoing consumer strength. Barclays upgrades its sector view on the U.S. autos and mobility to neutral from negative, as tariffs are now well understood by the market and likely to ease with additional trade deals. General Motors and Aptiv are especially well positioned, the analysts note, upgrading each to overweight from equal weight. GM should benefit from easing electric-vehicle regulations and resiliency in pricing, while parts supplier Aptiv can benefit from improved business dynamics alongside reasonable valuation, the analysts say. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1121 ET - A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery would likely clear regulatory scrutiny, according to Benchmark. The timing is "particularly auspicious" says analyst Daniel Kurnos. He says the FCC's September preliminary agenda seeks to potentially eliminate the rule prohibiting dual ownership of broadcast networks. While CBS is the only broadcast network and station group involved in this potential deal, it "suggests an openness to further media consolidation of the major players," Kurnos says. The Ellison family's close family ties with the Trump Administration will likely also help avoid potential obstacles to the merger, Benchmark says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1107 ET - Upwork has returned to growth in gross services volume, or the total amount of payments, fees and services processed on its platform, Citizens analysts say in a research note. The turnaround has been supported by product enhancements that appear to be driving up demand, the analysts say. Those include the rollout of Upwork's new variable freelancer fee, a revamped enterprise strategy, growth in AI-related work, AI-driven marketplace enhancements and sustained momentum in ads and monetization tools. CFO Erica Gessert said at a recent conference that the company is poised to start showing both revenue and GSV growth now and into 2026, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1433 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose for a third consecutive week, increasing by two to 416, or 72 fewer than a year ago, Baker Hughes reports. Rigs directed at natural gas were unchanged this week at 118, up by 21 from a year earlier. The EIA expects drilling activity in 2026 to be more centered in natural gas-intensive regions given its forecasts for lower oil but higher natural gas prices. "We forecast that crude oil will trade at its lowest premium to natural gas since 2005," the EIA said in its latest Short Term Energy Outlook. "We expect decreases in natural gas produced as a by-product of oil directed drilling will offset increases in that produced by natural gas-directed drilling." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1256 ET - The Renewable Fuels Association says that the September WASDE report from the USDA underscores the need for more ethanol production in the U.S.--with the USDA now projecting even more corn to be produced by farmers this year. "The supply of corn following this year's harvest is expected to significantly overwhelm demand, leaving 2.1 billion bushels of surplus," the RFA says. "This report again underscores the urgent need to quickly open and expand markets for corn, including removing unnecessary barriers that constrain market opportunities for ethanol." The RFA says that allowing E15 -or motor vehicle fuel cut with 15% ethanol- would help offset the huge surplus. CBOT corn is up 0.2% in afternoon trading, this after initially falling on the report's release. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1122 ET - European natural-gas prices are on track for a weekly gain of more than 2%, driven by concerns over potential supply disruptions amid heightened geopolitical tensions. In evening trade, the benchmark Dutch TTF contract rises 1.2% to 32.71 euros a megawatt hour. "The recent violation of Polish airspace by Russian drones has raised the prospect of the U.S. and Europe further restricting Moscow's energy exports," ANZ analysts say. According to media reports, the U.S. will urge its allies in the Group of Seven to impose tariffs on China and India for their purchases of Russian oil. On the other hand, EU gas stockpiles are steadily building, making investors confident that members of the bloc should reach their filling targets before the winter season begins. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1033 ET - Oil futures are recovering from the previous day's losses as the U.K. announces new sanctions on Russia and the U.S. urges Europe to put tariff pressure on buyers of Russian oil, namely India and China. "The Russia/Ukraine war remains a positive to crude prices with Ukraine hitting a major oil export facility in Russia late yesterday," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. "Russia exports nearly 7 million barrels a day, therefore any disruptions whether from tariffs or war conflicts could be a bullish catalyst for crude prices near-term." WTI is up 1.5% at $63.28 a barrel and Brent is up 1.6% at $67.43. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0954 ET - U.S. natural gas futures remain in a choppy trade around the $3 level with rally attempts held back by surplus inventories heading into the low-demand shoulder season. Yesterday's EIA report showed storage up 71 Bcf at 3,343 Bcf, or 188 Bcf above the five-year average. "A late-season hurricane or mild October pose risks for a storage overbuild and rapidly deteriorating gas prices," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. But "eventually, even normal October weather may allow a bullish weather-normalized supply/demand balance to rapidly winnow storage surpluses to the five-year norm." Nymex natural gas is up 2.1% at $2.995/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0908 ET - Equinor faces challenges from an expected decline in European gas prices and lower financial flexibility due to its participation in Orsted's rights issue, UBS analysts write. European gas storage has refilled steadily, while at the same time new LNG projects have started up. Longer term, LNG investments are picking up and UBS expects gas prices to drop even further than previously. Meanwhile, the Danish wind-farm developer has confirmed it will participate in the Orsted rights issue to maintain its 10% stake, at a cost of $939 million. "This will further increase gearing and reduce financial flexibility, increasing risks around shareholder returns into next year." UBS cuts its price target on the stock to 210 kroner ($21) from 220 kroner and reiterates its sell rating. Shares rise 0.5% to 242.50 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0820 ET - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, climb, as U.K. launches new sanctions on Russia. The U.K. foreign secretary Yvette Cooper on Friday announced new penalties on Russian companies and on ship transporting Russian oil. Investors are wary about escalating geopolitical risks and remain cautious on U.K. bonds due to concerns about the country's fiscal situation. The 10-year gilt yields rise around 4 basis points to last trade at 4.651%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0809 ET - Oil prices rebound in afternoon trade after the U.K. unveiled a fresh package of 100 sanctions targeting Russia's oil flows and military machine. Brent crude rises 2% to $67.68 a barrel, while WTI is up 1.8% to $63.48 a barrel after opening the session lower on oversupply concerns. Risks of disruption to Russian energy flows continue to underpin prices, despite bearish market fundamentals, analysts say. The U.K. measures come in response to the violation of NATO airspace over Poland earlier this week and recent strikes in Kyiv. Meanwhile, the EU is currently preparing a 19th package of sanctions against Russia and reportedly considering a faster phase-out of Russian fossil fuels. Two EU officials said President Trump asked the bloc to hit some goods from China and India--two major buyers of Russian oil--with tariffs of up to 100% to ratchet up pressure on Russia. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0529 ET - Weakness in the manufacturing sector held back U.K. GDP in July, economists at Investec Economics say in a note. U.K. manufacturing output dropped by 1.3% in July weighing on GDP growth which stagnated during the month. U.K. monthly GDP growth was flat in July, down from 0.4% growth in June.(miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0443 ET - Mining stocks have helped propel the FTSE 100 index to a record high in morning trade in London. Anglo American shares are up nearly 13% over the past 5 days on the news that it has agreed to merge with Teck Resources to create a $53 billion copper giant. Glencore is up 7%. Much of the share-price growth has been concentrated on gold and silver stocks as precious metals continue to benefit from safe-have demand. Fresnillo trades up 7%, Hochschild Mining is up 6%, with Endeavour Mining 4% higher. The FTSE 100 trades up 0.3% at 9,326 points. Over the year-to-date, it is up 14%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0409 ET - Oil prices fall in early trade, extending the previous session's losses as concerns over looming oversupply and softening U.S demand overshadow geopolitical risks in the Middle East and Eastern Europe. Brent crude slips 0.6% to $65.95 a barrel, while WTI is down 0.7% to $61.95 a barrel. The IEA now expects global supply to rise faster than previously forecast, far exceeding demand growth as OPEC+ continues to raise output. U.S. crude inventories also rose for a second straight week, underscoring softer demand in the world's largest consumer. The retreat follows days of gains fueled by prospects of stricter Western sanctions on Russian oil exports after President Trump asked the EU to hit some Chinese and Indian goods with tariffs in a new attempt to ratchet up pressure on the Kremlin. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0342 ET - European oil stocks slip in opening trade as worries about oversupply continue to mount. Brent crude trades down 0.7% at $65.93 a barrel and prices remain under pressure. A surprise inventory build of 3.9 million barrels in the U.S. last week combined with growing concerns around oversupply as OPEC+ members plan to boost production is contributing to traders' worries, Derren Nathan, head of equity research at Hargreaves Lansdown, writes. In London, BP shares fall 0.7% and Shell drops 0.15%. France's TotalEnergies is down 0.65% while shares in Spain's Repsol and Italy's Eni are down around 0.4%. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Pembina Pipeline on Track for Longest Winning Streak Since August 2024 — Data Talk
data.description:
Pembina Pipeline Corporation (PBA) is currently at $39.30, up $0.28 or 0.72%

  • Would be highest close since May 8, 2025, when it closed at $39.41
  • Currently up nine of the past 10 days

  • Best eight day stretch since the eight days ending Aug. 22, 2025, when it rose 6.79%
  • Up 3.48% this week; best weekly performance since the week ending Aug. 22, 2025, when it rose 3.85%

All data as of 2:31:04 PM ET

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: These Stocks Are Moving the Most Today: Warner Bros., Paramount Skydance, Tesla, Moderna, IonQ, Gemini Space, Microsoft, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Woelfel, Elsa Ohlen, and Mackenzie Tatananni

Stocks were mixed Friday after the Dow Jones Industrial Average crossed 46,000 for the first time Thursday and set a record, joining the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite at all-time closing highs following U.S. inflation data that likely won't prevent the Federal Reserve from cutting interest rates when it meets next week.

These stocks were making moves Friday:

Warner Bros. Discovery soared 19% following the stock's surge Thursday of 29% — its largest daily percentage increase on record — after The Wall Street Journal reported Paramount Skydance was preparing a takeover bid that would be backed by the Ellison family. Paramount's majority cash bid would be for the entire company including its cable networks and movie studio, the Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Paramount, meanwhile, was up 5.8% at $18.47. Earlier in the session the stock set a 52-week high of $18.56.

Tesla rose 7.1%. The move followed a gain of 6% on Thursday for shares of the electric-vehicle maker, which rose in part on U.S. inflation data that was in line with economists' estimates and firmed up the belief the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates. Since cars are generally financed, auto companies benefit from lower rates.

IonQ surged 18% to $55.40, on pace for a record high, according to Dow Jones Market Data. The quantum computing company said it had secured regulatory approval from the UK Investment Security Unit for its acquisition of Oxford Ionics, a British start-up.

Super Micro Computer rose 3.1% after beginning shipments of its artificial-intelligence Blackwell Ultra solutions powered by Nvidia chips to customers worldwide. The systems includes Nvidia's plug-and-play HGX B300 system and GB300 NVL72, which are used for training AI models. Nvidia was up 0.4%.

Adobe was down 0.6%. The software company posted fiscal third-quarter earnings and revenue that beat Wall Street estimates, and issued forecasts for the fourth quarter and fiscal year that also topped expectations. Adobe said 99% of Fortune 100 companies have used artificial intelligence in an Adobe app, and more than 40% of its top 50 enterprise accounts doubled their annualized recurring revenue spending since the start of fiscal 2023. Shares are down 22% this year.

Moderna tumbled 7.2% after the Washington Post reported that Trump-appointed health officials were planning to link Covid-19 vaccines to 25 child deaths. Officials could include the claim in a presentation to an expert panel of CDC advisers next week, the Post reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Gemini Space Station surged 43%. The stock made its trading debut Friday at $37.01, up 32% from its initial public offering price of $28. Gemini is a cryptocurrency brokerage firm run by twins Cameron and Tyler Winklevoss.

Figma, an Adobe rival, was down 4.4% following a 9.6% jump Thursday. A deal for Adobe to acquire the software maker for $20 billion fell through in 2022 on regulatory concerns, particularly from the European Commission and the U.K. Competition and Markets Authority.

Microsoft rose 2.1% after reaching a deal to extend its partnership with OpenAI. The current AI investment boom began with ChatGPT's debut in late 2022, and it wouldn't have been possible without funding from Microsoft and access to its Azure cloud. But the relationship has frayed, as both companies' AI ambitions outgrew their partnership.

RH was down 4.9% after the home furnishings retailer's second-quarter earnings widely missed consensus estimates and revenue came up short of Wall Street targets. The company also reduced its current fiscal-year revenue outlook, saying it expects revenue growth of 9% to 11%, down from its prior guidance of 10% to 13%. RH said its updated outlook "reflects a $30 million cost of incremental tariffs, net of mitigation, in the second half."

Figure Technology Solutions was rising 6.6% to $33.17. Shares of the company, which uses blockchain technology to help generate home equity lines of credit, opened Thursday at $36 and ended the session at $31.11, a gain of 24% over its initial public offering price of $25.

Shares of Opendoor Technologies fell 12% after the real estate technology platform closed Thursday at $10.48, up 79% during the session, marking the stock's largest daily percentage increase on record. Driving the stock higher was the naming of Shopify Chief Operating Officer Kaz Nejatian as its new chief executive and reappointing co-founders Keith Rabois and Eric Wu to the board.

Write to Joe Woelfel at joseph.woelfel@barrons.com and Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Thales, Airbus, Leonardo eye initial agreement on 10 bln-euro satellite JV, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • 'Project Bromo' seeks to counter competition from China, U.S
  • Negotiations have regained momentum after stalling, sources say
  • Political approval and final ownership details are still pending

Europe's aerospace groups Leonardo, Thales and Airbus have redoubled efforts to combine their satellite businesses into a 10 billion-euro ($11.7 billion) joint venture and are working towards hammering out an initial agreement in coming weeks, sources close to the matter told Reuters in recent days.

Under "Project Bromo," named after an Indonesian volcano, the three companies plan to set up a satellite manufacturing company to compete with rivals from China and the U.S., including Elon Musk's Starlink. Talks have regained momentum after a rough period in the summer when the parties could not agree on governance and valuation, stalling an agreement, three sources said. The talks looked at risk of collapsing, according to another source.

A memorandum of understanding could now be signed by the end of September, though the timing could slip, the sources said.

Political signals so far have been encouraging, but details of ownership and governance must be signed by nations involved, three of the sources said. The venture is expected to include dedicated entities to protect sensitive national interests.

An agreement is not guaranteed, and talks could still fall apart, the sources warned.

The exact holdings of the new space venture are still being negotiated, the sources said, adding that ownership of the venture could be split roughly in three equal parts.

The new satellite venture value of about 10 billion euros reflects the units’ combined revenue of 6 billion-6.5 billion euros and sector peers, which trade at 1.5-3 times revenue, the two sources said.

A spokesperson for Leonardo declined to comment.

Airbus referred to comments from CEO Guillaume Faury, who said earlier this week the companies were moving towards a deal and talking to European governments on the venture. “We are on the way,” he told a group of reporters in Washington.

“We have started also to communicate with all stakeholders and we have started to try for anti-trust (approval), so we are in the process of doing it,” Faury added.

Thales said: "No agreement has been reached at this stage. We are continuing our work. Any further comment would be premature."

Preliminary attempts over the past decade to create a European satellite champion foundered partly on anti-trust concerns and national rivalries. But the dramatic rise of Starlink and a shift in the market towards cheaper, low-orbit satellites have increased pressure on Europe’s main suppliers to combine assets or be pushed out of the market.

Talks to reshape the industry's structure are part of a broader attempt by Europe to boost sovereign capabilities as geopolitical tensions have mounted and U.S. policies have shifted.

All three companies have minority government shareholders and analysts say any agreement involving sensitive assets and technology would need political approval.

Italy's industry ministry was not immediately available for comment. Germany's defense ministry did not immediately respond. A spokesperson for France's state shareholding agency APE declined to comment.

A potential deal would create a standalone European satellite champion, based broadly on missile maker MBDA, owned by Airbus, Leonardo and BAE Systems. MBDA was founded in 2001 through the merger of Anglo-French Matra BAe Dynamics, France's Aerospatiale Matra Missiles and missile activities of Anglo-Italian Alenia Marconi Systems.

Reuters reported in June that Leonardo and Thales current joint ventures - Thales Alenia Space and Telespazio - would be included in the structure.

($1 = 0.8536 euros)

data.title: Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery Integration Could Take Years — Market Talk
data.description: A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery makes sense strategically, but a full integration would take time, BofA Securities analysts say in a note. They estimate that integration and restructuring under the combined company would likely take years to implement, pointing to the lengthy process that came with Warner Bros.' combination with Discovery. That said, the deal would significantly boost Paramount's prospects, particularly in film and television, given access to Warner Bros.' best-in-class library, they say. It would also create a formidable competitor in streaming with the potential combination of Paramount+ and HBO Max, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1426 ET - A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery makes sense strategically, but a full integration would take time, BofA Securities analysts say in a note. They estimate that integration and restructuring under the combined company would likely take years to implement, pointing to the lengthy process that came with Warner Bros.' combination with Discovery. That said, the deal would significantly boost Paramount's prospects, particularly in film and television, given access to Warner Bros.' best-in-class library, they say. It would also create a formidable competitor in streaming with the potential combination of Paramount+ and HBO Max, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1419 ET - 2025 will mark the end of one of Bank of America's biggest investment themes for this decade: the trend of moving away from Chinese assets known as the "Anywhere But China" trade, the bank's analysts say in a research note. Amid the AI bubble in the U.S., investors should take a "barbell" approach to investing, with capital flowing to a mix of low-risk and high-risk global technology investments, the analysts say. China and Japan are bright spots in this new paradigm, they say. Chinese technology companies are an optimal way to barbell the U.S. AI bubble, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1312 ET - Most of Boeing's 3,200 St. Louis-area union machinists voted Friday to keep picketing, rejecting a new five-year contract proposal that their negotiators had agreed to earlier in the week. "Our members will always have the final say in their futures," union representative Tom Boelling said. The vote adds pressure on Boeing's defense segment, which works on fighter jets and munitions in the region. ( andrew.fitzgerald@wsj.com ; @drewfitzgerald)

1211 ET - Amazon Autos hasn't hit top gear yet, especially in used vehicles, say Davidson analysts Tom White and Wyatt Swanson. Amazon Autos was launched in 2023 with a partnership with Hyundai for new vehicles, but the analysts say that Hyundai dealers are still seeing very limited sales through Amazon. Early last month, Amazon said dealers would be able to list their used vehicles on Amazon Autos, but " there remains relatively scant Used inventory available on the Amazon Autos platform," the analysts say. Almost all of the used inventory that is available is from Hertz, which recently said it was partnering with Amazon, and participating Hyundai dealers, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1156 ET - The estimated funding level of pension plans sponsored by S&P 1500 companies increased 1 percentage point in August to 109% as a result of an increase in domestic equities, according to consulting firm Mercer LLC. As of the end of August, the plans' estimated aggregate surplus increased by $20 billion, to $136 billion, compared with a $116 billion surplus at the end of July, Mercer said. "We saw the interest rate curve steepen, with long-term rates rising and short-term rates falling, which could have various impacts on companies within the S&P 1500 depending on the duration of their plans," said Mercer partner Scott Jarboe. (jennifer.williams@wsj.com; @jenkayw)

1126 ET - Tariffs were expected to hit the auto industry hard, "yet six months into the onset of tariffs, we've been positively surprised by the extent to which the industry has held in better than anticipated," Barclays analysts Dan Levy and Josh Cho say in a research note, citing minimal disruption to production and sales, as well as ongoing consumer strength. Barclays upgrades its sector view on the U.S. autos and mobility to neutral from negative, as tariffs are now well understood by the market and likely to ease with additional trade deals. General Motors and Aptiv are especially well positioned, the analysts note, upgrading each to overweight from equal weight. GM should benefit from easing electric-vehicle regulations and resiliency in pricing, while parts supplier Aptiv can benefit from improved business dynamics alongside reasonable valuation, the analysts say. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1121 ET - A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery would likely clear regulatory scrutiny, according to Benchmark. The timing is "particularly auspicious" says analyst Daniel Kurnos. He says the FCC's September preliminary agenda seeks to potentially eliminate the rule prohibiting dual ownership of broadcast networks. While CBS is the only broadcast network and station group involved in this potential deal, it "suggests an openness to further media consolidation of the major players," Kurnos says. The Ellison family's close family ties with the Trump Administration will likely also help avoid potential obstacles to the merger, Benchmark says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1107 ET - Upwork has returned to growth in gross services volume, or the total amount of payments, fees and services processed on its platform, Citizens analysts say in a research note. The turnaround has been supported by product enhancements that appear to be driving up demand, the analysts say. Those include the rollout of Upwork's new variable freelancer fee, a revamped enterprise strategy, growth in AI-related work, AI-driven marketplace enhancements and sustained momentum in ads and monetization tools. CFO Erica Gessert said at a recent conference that the company is poised to start showing both revenue and GSV growth now and into 2026, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1040 ET - A Paramount Skydance/Warner Bros. Discovery merger "could yield meaningful upside for the combined entity," says Benchmark analyst Daniel Kurnos. Two years ago, Benchmark estimated a merger between the two companies would create synergies between $3.15 billion and $4.9 billion, although the companies have since begun their own cost reduction programs. "The math remains relatively the same, and the industrial logic has only gotten stronger in what appears to be a consolidating media landscape," Kurnos says. The analyst adds that even without synergies, "Paramount could pay $18 per share for Warner and still have the deal be value neutral or possibly even marginally accretive." Warner Bros. Discovery surges 12%, while Paramount Skydance is up 4%. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1022 ET - Six Flags' mid-season update was generally in-line with expectations, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. Attendance trends have improved throughout the summer, though it appears that at least some of the increase is due to discounting and promotions, as revenue and in-park spending are down. Looking forward, increasing consumer demand around Halloween events could help boost results in the back half of the year, the analysts say. But while the amusement-park operator also noted that 2026 season pass sales are tracking "well ahead" of last year, the analysts remain cautious on the stock due to "the uncertainty around leadership and therefore strategic direction for the time being." Shares rise 7.4%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1017 ET - Kroger has a lot of projects on its shopping list, and the grocer is executing them well, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a research note. The company notes improving volumes in its latest quarter as it works to maintain low prices, and as consumers buy ingredients to cook at home more often. "But at the same time, the company noted to us in our follow up call that many of the current initiatives (a) are shorter term in nature, (b) were items that would probably have been addressed sooner if not for the prior focus on the merger with Albertsons, and (c) were probably well underway when the prior CEO abruptly departed in March," the analysts warn. "So, it does seem like there are still some longer term aspects of grocers strategy that could be re-visited." (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1006 ET - Roughly half--48%--of newly built apartments completed in 1Q were rented within three months, according to Redfin. That is up from 47% in 4Q 2024 and 46% the quarter before that. While the recent uptick in the rental absorption rate is small, it's notable because for much of 2021-2023, the absorption rate was declining. It's likely inching up now because the number of new apartments hitting the market is falling. Renters have fewer options to choose from, making it easier for landlords to fill units fast. As many Americans find themselves priced out of homeownership, rents are beginning to rise again. Apartment building permits have fallen below pre-pandemic levels. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

data.title: Ticket reseller StubHub's IPO 20 times oversubscribed, source says
data.description:
Ticket reseller StubHub, which delayed its U.S. listing in April amid market volatility, has drawn over 20 times as many orders for its planned initial public offering as there are available shares, a source familiar with the matter said on Friday.

The outsized demand for the IPO, which is set to price shares on Tuesday, reflects pent-up investor appetite for tech-heavy consumer platforms.

StubHub did not immediately respond to Reuters' request for comment.

Madrone Partners-backed StubHub had postponed its IPO roadshow earlier this year as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs roiled global markets and froze all dealmaking.

In a stark turn of events, U.S. IPOs have had a triumphant week as companies ranging from crypto to consumer tapped the public markets amid record high equity markets and easing tariff worries.

Swedish buy-now, pay-later firm Klarna's shares jumped in its long-awaited debut earlier this week, reinforcing bullish investor sentiments for fintechs.

Separately, cafe chain Black Rock Coffee Bar's (BRCB.O) shares rose above their issue price earlier in the day, as investors brushed off residual concerns over the consumer company's health.

StubHub is targeting a valuation of up to $9.2 billion in its U.S. IPO, seeking to raise up to $851 million by offering 34 million shares priced between $22 and $25 each.

The company was co-founded in 2000 by Jeff Fluhr and current CEO Eric Baker, who had exited StubHub ahead of its $310-million sale to e-commerce firm eBay in 2007.

Baker in 2006 launched viagogo, a rival ticket reseller in Europe, which in 2020 bought StubHub from eBay for $4.05 billion.

J.P. Morgan and Goldman Sachs are the lead underwriters of the offering.

StubHub will list on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol "STUB".

data.title: Front and Center in This Week's IPOs: Individual Investors — WSJ
data.description:
By Corrie Driebusch and Hannah Erin Lang

Everyday investors typically have access to a tiny fraction of the shares sold in an initial public offering. The cryptocurrency platform Gemini Space Station, which debuted Friday, earmarked nearly a third of its shares for such buyers.

So-called retail investors are getting bigger allotments than ever before in IPOs. Companies are glad that it generates buzz, while bankers say it can stabilize a company's share price. The latter issue is top of mind after several of this year's biggest IPOs were plagued by big price jumps on their first trading days, which sounds good but often means money was left on the table.

Institutional investors buy the bulk of shares in IPOs, while individuals usually get access to around 6% of the shares sold. Most of the major debuts this week have aimed for well above that.

Gemini, a crypto exchange controlled by the Winklevoss twins, planned to allocate up to 30% of its IPO to retail investors. Its shares opened up 32% at $37.01 on Friday, giving the company a market value of more than $4 billion.

Klarna, a buy-now-pay-later company, sold more than 10% of its offering to retail investors in its Wednesday debut, some of the people familiar with the matter said.

Its shares closed up 15% on their first day of trading, which is about what bankers facilitating IPOs typically target. Anything much higher suggests the IPO was underpriced. Klarna is now worth around $16 billion.

Bankers began fretting over too much demand for new issues after the stablecoin issuer Circle Internet Group's stock more than doubled on its first day of trading in June. Then, Figma, the software company, saw its stock soar 250% in its July debut. That meant the company and investors who sold shares in the IPO missed out on roughly $3 billion.

Many blamed the exuberance on individual investors with little price sensitivity flooding into shares. The idea now is to let more of them in from the beginning.

A big first-day pop can also mess with a company's carefully curated investor base. Executives handpick big institutional investors to allocate shares to in an IPO, favoring fund managers likely to hold stock for a long time, such as Fidelity or T. Rowe Price Group. If the stock doubles or triples within the first few weeks of trading, those funds might sell to lock in gains, meaning that careful curation was all for naught.

Retail investors, meanwhile, are more likely to hold on to their shares, which is another reason including them can damp share-price volatility in the early days.

Part of the investor enthusiasm is due to the IPO market's having just snapped back this summer from a three-year lull.

The Trump administration has been considering an offering of the mortgage giants Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac this year, The Wall Street Journal reported last month. Should the offering come together, it could be among the largest of all time.

Robinhood Markets Chief Executive Officer Vlad Tenev has long advocated for individual investors to get bigger pieces of public offerings. When his own company went public in 2021, it set aside a quarter of shares for the retail crowd. Back then, Tenev said, he was met with skepticism from Wall Street. But things have shifted.

Companies now "understand that having a large and vibrant retail shareholder base is good for business," the brokerage CEO said in an interview. This week, Robinhood customers could request shares of companies including Klarna, Gemini and StubHub, which is set to start trading next week.

Angel Studios, an independent studio that makes values-driven movies and television shows, built a base of roughly 70,000 individual investors through crowdfunding ahead of its stock-market debut Thursday. Angel went public by merging with a special-purpose acquisition company. The company also counts big tech investors including Gigafund as backers.

The cryptocurrency exchange Bullish allocated 20% of shares to individual investors when it went public last month. Bullish's shares still rose 84% on their first day.

Its debut was closely scrutinized because its chief executive is the IPO veteran Tom Farley, the former president of the New York Stock Exchange. Farley has said he anticipated the volatility, and people close to the deal said they believe the big allocation to individual investors helped ward off an even bigger first-day pop.

Since their debuts, shares of Bullish, Circle and Figma have all retreated from their early highs.

Write to Corrie Driebusch at corrie.driebusch@wsj.com and Hannah Erin Lang at hannaherin.lang@wsj.com

data.title: Jamf rises after Reuters report on it exploring sale
data.description:
** Shares of device management software provider Jamf Holding rise 5% to $11.08

** Vista Equity-backed JAMF is exploring a sale as weakness in its stock price makes it a prime takeover candidate for other companies, Reuters reports

** JAMF is working with Citigroup bankers on the sale

** Vista Equity, which owns 34% of JAMF'S stock, is expected to exit its position as part of the sale

** Citi and Vista declined to comment, while Jamf did not respond to a request

** JAMF had fallen 25% YTD, as of last close

data.title: Neil Young hit with trademark lawsuit over band name Chrome Hearts
data.description:
Key points:
  • Fashion brand Chrome Hearts filed suit in Los Angeles
  • It seeks money damages and order blocking use of name

Rock legend Neil Young and his latest band the Chrome Hearts have been sued for trademark infringement by Los Angeles luxury fashion brand Chrome Hearts.

The lawsuit, filed on Thursday in federal court in Los Angeles, said the band's name and merchandise will confuse consumers into believing they are connected with the fashion brand. The complaint sought an unspecified amount of monetary damages and a judicial order blocking Young from using the name Chrome Hearts.

Representatives for Young at his label Warner Records did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Friday. Attorneys and spokespeople for Chrome Hearts also did not respond to requests for comment.

The Canadian-born Young, 79, was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame as a solo artist in 1995 and as a member of the band Buffalo Springfield in 1997. He was also a member of the supergroup Crosby, Stills, Nash & Young and has toured for decades with backing from the band Crazy Horse.

Young announced a tour with a new backing band called the Chrome Hearts last year. The fashion brand Chrome Hearts told the court that the band's name has confused vendors into thinking that the band's merchandise was part of a collaboration.

The brand said it has previously collaborated with musicians including the Rolling Stones and Drake, which it said increased the risk of confusion from Young's band name. The brand said it sent Young's representatives a cease-and-desist letter in July, but the band has since continued to tour under the Chrome Hearts name.

Young's band is scheduled to play in Mountain View, California, on Friday and at the Hollywood Bowl in Los Angeles on Monday.

data.title: Grain Traders See Short-Covering Opportunity After WASDE — Market Talk
data.description: This afternoon's USDA WASDE report made only minor adjustments to the record corn forecasts introduced by the agency last month, keeping U.S. corn supply projections onerous. But CBOT corn futures are now leading grain futures higher in late trading, even with the USDA's projections not seen as supportive for prices. "On the surface yield, production and ending stocks numbers are bearish-especially with the market having rallied going into the report," says Joel Karlin of Ocean State Research. But traders seem to be instead taking the opportunity to cover on the net-short position seen in corn among managed-money firms--which Karlin says may be because traders believe that corn futures have established their low for the year. CBOT corn is up 2.2%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)
